findings.title: Metrics METRICS_TOKEN comparison uses non-constant-time string equality
findings.description: The METRICS_TOKEN check on line 33 uses the JavaScript `!==` string operator, which short-circuits on the first differing character and is therefore vulnerable to timing side-channels. An attacker with a high-resolution clock or many samples could statistically determine the token value one character at a time. main.ts already defines a `safeEqual` helper using `crypto.timingSafeEqual`, but it is not imported or used here.
findings.root_cause: Direct string comparison (`!==`) instead of a constant-time comparison function such as `crypto.timingSafeEqual`.
findings.impact: A determined attacker with access to the metrics endpoint can exploit differential timing to brute-force the METRICS_TOKEN, gaining unauthenticated read access to internal Prometheus metrics (request counts, routes, latencies — useful for profiling the API internals).
findings.suggested_fix: Import `timingSafeEqual` from `crypto` (or re-export `safeEqual` from main.ts into a utility), then replace the `authorization !== expected` check with `timingSafeEqual(Buffer.from(authorization), Buffer.from(expected))` after verifying both buffers have the same length.
acceptance_criteria: Token comparison uses crypto.timingSafeEqual or equivalent constant-time function
A buffer-length mismatch (different-length token) is handled before calling timingSafeEqual to avoid the 'buffers must be equal length' exception
findings.notes: safeEqual() is already implemented in main.ts line 34-38 using timingSafeEqual — it just needs to be extracted to a shared utility and used here.

findings.title: Prometheus metric labels built from unsanitized req.path — label injection possible
findings.description: The Prometheus text-format label string on line 37 is assembled by direct template interpolation of `route` (which equals `req.path` from the interceptor, line 34 of metrics.interceptor.ts). The `req.path` value comes from the raw request URL path and is not sanitized. A request whose path contains `"` or `}` will corrupt the label grammar, e.g. GET /api/test"} injected{x="1 produces the line `http_requests_total{method="GET",route="/api/test"} injected{x="1",status="200"} 1`. This is exploitable for Prometheus scraper confusion and could, in edge scenarios, overwrite metric values in Prometheus.
findings.root_cause: Route label value is taken verbatim from `req.path` and interpolated into the Prometheus text format without escaping double quotes or other special characters.
findings.impact: An attacker can craft requests whose path corrupts the `/api/metrics` output, confusing Prometheus scrapers. At minimum this causes Prometheus parse errors; in sophisticated scenarios it can inject arbitrary label key-value pairs into metric exposition.
findings.suggested_fix: Escape double-quote characters in all label values before building the label string, or use an established Prometheus client library (prom-client) that handles escaping automatically. At minimum: `route.replace(/\\/g, '\\\\').replace(/"/g, '\\"').replace(/\n/g, '\\n')`.
acceptance_criteria: A request path containing '"' or '}' does not break Prometheus text format output
Unit test for renderMetrics with a label containing special characters passes
findings.notes: The Prometheus text format spec (https://prometheus.io/docs/instrumenting/exposition_formats/) requires that label values escape backslash, double-quote, and newline characters.

findings.title: UpdateSettingDto.value and .description lack @MaxLength — unbounded strings stored to DB
findings.description: The `value` and `description` fields in UpdateSettingDto have `@IsString()` and `@IsNotEmpty()` but no `@MaxLength()`. An authenticated user with `settings:update` permission (admin-tier) can submit arbitrarily long strings that are persisted to the appSettings table. Although `settings:update` is a privileged permission, defence-in-depth requires bounding free-text inputs at the DTO layer to prevent accidental or deliberate memory/storage exhaustion. The global ValidationPipe bodyLimit of 1 MiB provides a coarse backstop, but no per-field cap exists.
findings.root_cause: Missing @MaxLength decorator on `value` and `description` fields in UpdateSettingDto.
findings.impact: A privilege-abusing admin can store very large blobs in appSettings (up to the 1 MiB body limit per request), potentially causing DB bloat and excessive memory use when settings are loaded at module init (initializeDefaultSettings iterates all keys).
findings.suggested_fix: Add `@MaxLength(10000)` to `value` and `@MaxLength(500)` to `description` in UpdateSettingDto. Adjust limits based on realistic maximum setting value sizes.
acceptance_criteria: Sending a value longer than 10000 chars to PUT /settings/:key returns 400
Sending a description longer than 500 chars returns 400
findings.notes: The settings:update permission is admin-only; severity is nit rather than blocking because of this privilege requirement.

findings.title: BulkUpdateSettingsDto.settings has no entry-count cap — unbounded bulk writes possible
findings.description: The BulkUpdateSettingsDto accepts a `settings` object typed as `Record<string, any>` with only `@IsObject()` validation. There is no cap on the number of entries. The bulkUpdate service method iterates every entry sequentially (settings.service.ts line 273), issuing one upsert per key. An attacker with `settings:update` permission could submit an object with thousands of keys, causing a long-running sequential DB write loop and potential denial-of-service. The isKnownKey check in the service rejects unknown keys, but the iteration still happens for each entry before the check short-circuits.
findings.root_cause: No `@ArrayMaxSize` or `@ValidateBy` constraint limiting the number of keys in the `settings` map.
findings.impact: Authenticated admin-tier users can trigger a long sequential upsert loop, degrading API response times for other requests.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a custom validator or validate inside the DTO to ensure `Object.keys(settings).length <= 20` (matching the known DEFAULT_SETTINGS count). Alternatively, add an early guard in `bulkUpdate`: `if (Object.keys(settings).length > 20) throw new BadRequestException('Too many settings')`.
acceptance_criteria: POST /settings/bulk with ≤20 known keys continues to work
findings.notes: Severity limited to nit because settings:update is admin-only; an admin deliberately abusing this is a trust boundary issue more than an external security threat.

verified_clean: apps/api/src/analytics/analytics.controller.ts — all routes have @RequirePermissions; uses @CurrentUser(); no raw SQL
apps/api/src/analytics/analytics.service.ts — all Prisma queries parameterized; user scope enforced via AccessScopeService.projectScopeWhere; no hardcoded secrets
apps/api/src/analytics/dto/analytics-query.dto.ts — @IsEnum and @IsUUID on all fields; whitelist/forbidNonWhitelisted enforced globally
apps/api/src/analytics/advanced/analytics-advanced.controller.ts — all 6 routes have @RequirePermissions('reports:view'); @CurrentUser() passed to service
apps/api/src/analytics/advanced/services/snapshots-query.service.ts — projectIds from query filtered through DB scope (AND [projectScope, archivedClause]) before use; no IDOR
apps/api/src/analytics/advanced/services/workload.service.ts — accessScope.projectScopeWhere called; limit bounded to @Max(100) in DTO
apps/api/src/analytics/advanced/services/milestones-completion.service.ts — project scope enforced; no raw SQL; no secret logging
apps/api/src/analytics/advanced/services/tasks-breakdown.service.ts — user-supplied projectIds intersected with projectScope (not trusted alone); scope enforced
apps/api/src/analytics/advanced/services/recent-activity.service.ts — accessScope.projectScopeWhere; days bounded @Max(365); no secret logging
apps/api/src/analytics/advanced/snapshot-scheduler.service.ts — Redis leader lock prevents duplicate runs; no hardcoded secrets; Redis password via ConfigService
apps/api/src/settings/settings.controller.ts — all routes have @RequirePermissions; settings:read for reads, settings:update for mutations
apps/api/src/settings/settings.service.ts — update() enforces isKnownKey allowlist; findByCategory() validates against AppSettingsCategory enum; all Prisma queries parameterized
apps/api/src/metrics/metrics.module.ts — MetricsController marked @Public; token guard handled in controller
